projects.covert.title: site.hopen
projects.covert.title.text
site.hclose
site.br
site.popen
Prior to 2008 my requirement for virtualisation was quite modest. For one thing, my hardware could not run any significant load.
For another, I really only required it for the purpose of running one or two windows applications, on rare occasions, and for testing
new Slackware distributions prior to deployment or upgrade.
site.pclose
site.br
site.popen
When I took a position with "Cray, Australia", I was unable to work on my customer's site until I had been inducted and, as a consequence,
spent a few months in the office with not set schedule of work. I set about building some clusters of VMs so that I could learn how the PBS
Scheduler works, and I also tried to get some mpich-based cluster applications working. It became apparent, very quickly, that separate scripts
would not 'cut it'. I realised, later, that a static config with a shell or awk script was also not ideal. Thus was born the idea for COVERT, though,
that name did not precipitate until some years later — a tongue in cheek dig at my job role at the time.
site.pclose
site.br
projects.covert.title.text: Chris' Own Virtualisation Enviroment for Research and Testing (COVERT)

site.popen
From its inception, COVERT existed to maintain configuration and to manage the complexity around Qemu command-line arguments. Although it has grown
somewhat organically, there have always been goals in pursuit of:
site.pclose
site.br
projects.covert.overview.5: Preservation of configuration and operational provenance
